content.problemStatement:
Cyael is a teacher at a very famous school in Byteland and she is known by her students for being very polite to them and also to encourage them to get good marks on their tests.

Then, if they get good marks she will reward them with candies :) However, she knows they are all very good at Mathematics, so she decided to split the candies evenly to all the students she considers worth of receiving them, so they don't fight with each other. 

She has a bag which initially contains **N** candies and she intends to split the candies evenly to **K** students. To do this she will proceed as follows: while she has more than **K** candies she will give exactly 1 candy to each student until she has less than **K** candies. On this situation, as she can't split candies equally among all students she will keep the remaining candies to herself.

Your job is to tell how many candies will each student and the teacher
receive after the splitting is performed.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers N and K, where N is the number of candies in the bag and K is the number of students.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line with two space‑separated integers: the number of candies each student receives and the number of candies the teacher keeps.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test: 10/2 = 5 each, remainder 0. Second test: 100/3 = 33 each, remainder 1.
There are no students, so each student gets 0 and the teacher keeps all 5 candies.
No candies to distribute; each student gets 0 and the teacher gets 0.

solution.approach: For each test case we need to distribute candies one by one to all K students while possible. This is exactly integer division: each student receives floor(N / K) candies and the teacher keeps the remainder N % K. The only special case is K = 0 – there are no students, so every student gets 0 candies and the teacher keeps all N candies. The algorithm is O(1) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few variables are used.
